reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child-centred comprehensive schooling (Kockás könyv, 2016); teacher autonomy in assessment design; equity of access to high-quality peer learning; reduction of test-preparation stress on age-10 children
reactions.interest: Pupils in lower-SES and rural families; teachers tasked with inclusive assessment rather than gatekeeping; school networks serving dispersed populations
reactions.public_good_frame: Reducing the SES-sorting machine at its entry point strengthens equal opportunity and child wellbeing by delaying high-stakes stratification and weakening the test-prep arms race.
reactions.argument: S1 addresses the root mechanism we object to: high-stakes written selection that favours coached, affluent children. A redesigned assessment that resists coaching directly serves our interest in child-centred learning and lower selection stress. However, the scenario's own evidence status (mixed; SES-proxy contested; coaching-resistance not yet established in Hungary) means the equity gain is contingent, not assured. The interest we represent—lower-SES pupils' access to peers and teachers trained for heterogeneity—gains only if the workable SES indicator actually exists and outreach lifts application rates. We cannot endorse a reform whose equity mechanism depends on unproven instruments. Additionally, if reformed channels become scarcer, informal selection (interviews, reputation) may reproduce SES sorting by another route, defeating our purpose.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes unambiguous if: (a) a pilot demonstrates a psychometrically valid SES proxy with low gaming (test against a comparison group to show proxy resistance to income-declaration manipulation); (b) randomized coaching studies show the new assessment resists preparation at least 40% more effectively than the current exam (measured via item-level contamination analysis); (c) outreach evaluation in a pilot settlement shows uptake gains for low-SES families of at least 15 percentage points. Opposition would harden if the scenario proceeds without pilot evidence on these three mechanisms.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Delayed sorting to age 14 reduces early SES-based separation (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006); teacher autonomy in heterogeneous classes; school networks serving rural populations; child wellbeing through later high-stakes differentiation
reactions.interest: Pupils in lower-SES and rural families; teachers in general-school upper grades receiving higher-achieving peers later; rural schools retaining high-achievers longer to maintain network viability
reactions.public_good_frame: Progressively delaying tracking to age 14 reduces early SES-based sorting and allows more children to spend their formative years in heterogeneous peer groups, strengthening both equity and rural school sustainability.
reactions.argument: S2 directly serves our core value: delayed sorting reduces the SES-gradient window and keeps more high-achievers in general schools longer, which benefits rural networks and creates more inclusive upper-grade peer groups. The evidence on earlier tracking and larger SES gradients is strong (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006). However, the scenario's internal logic is internally tensioned: it caps state selective places but assumes non-state maintainers will not absorb displaced demand—an assumption the scenario itself flags as 'directly contradicted' by political risks. If church and foundation schools expand their selective intake proportionally, the cap becomes merely a reallocation, not a reduction in early sorting. We cannot support a reform that appears to reduce sorting but merely pushes it into non-state channels where we have less visibility over admissions equity. Additionally, the scenario flags the residual selective seats becoming 'even more SES-skewed' absent an S1-type rule change—we will not accept concentration of remaining seats as the price of capping numbers.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes unambiguous if: (a) a binding regulatory agreement with church and foundation maintainers (published and audited annually) shows that selective intake at non-state schools does not rise as state selective places fall (measure via Oktatási Hivatal registry data: non-state selective places as % of total selective places must not rise above baseline in any pilot year); (b) the scenario is bundled with an S1-type admission-rule change for residual selective seats to prevent concentration; (c) longitudinal peer-effect evaluation in pilot upper-grade cohorts shows measurable gains in achievement and engagement for lower-SES pupils retained in general schools. Opposition hardens if the cap is enacted without maintainer agreements or if residual seats visibly become more SES-skewed during the pilot.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Comprehensive schooling to age 14 as core model (Kockás könyv, 2016); delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome gradients (Pekkarinen, Uusitalo & Kerr 2009; Jakubowski et al. 2016); teacher professional autonomy in differentiated instruction; child-centred learning in heterogeneous classes; school network sustainability
reactions.interest: Pupils from lower-SES and rural families; teachers trained and supported for inclusive, differentiated instruction; rural school networks serving mixed-ability cohorts; the teaching profession as a stable, respected career
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural abolition of age-10/12 selection and transition to comprehensive lower-secondary schooling, bundled with teacher investment, is the only reform that addresses the root sorting mechanism rather than compensating around it. It creates equitable peer environments for all children during their most formative years and restores teacher autonomy in mixed-ability instruction.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that structurally dismantles the SES-sorting engine we oppose. Delaying tracking to age 14 has strong international evidence (Finland, Poland) showing reduced SES-outcome gradients. Critically, bundling it with standardized teacher training and diagnostic support (not performance audits or compliance benchmarks) prevents within-class variance from driving middle-class flight—this addresses the legitimate concern that comprehensive schooling without teacher investment fails. The scenario's largest risks—middle-class exit and sorting migration to non-state schools—are real, but they are political durability challenges, not structural flaws in the model itself. We note the scenario assumes political ownership survives two electoral cycles, which it acknowledges is contradicted by feasibility analysis. That is a serious constraint, but it is a governance problem, not a problem with the educational logic. We also note that teacher shortage is a current crisis, not a reform-specific barrier: without comprehensive reform, teacher burnout in selective schools and salary-driven exit in general schools will worsen. S3 at least couples structural change with teacher investment; the alternatives do not. We support this scenario with the clear expectation that success depends entirely on (a) multi-year teacher retraining in differentiated instruction before comprehensive rollout, (b) early diagnostic support staffed and funded at scale, and (c) binding admissions rules for non-state schools to prevent exit-driven sorting relocation.
reactions.condition_to_change: Unambiguous support is contingent on: (a) demonstration pilots in 5–8 diverse settlements (urban mixed-SES, urban disadvantaged, rural) running for 3+ years showing at least modest equity gains (SES-outcome gradient narrowing by 10%+ or high-achiever retention in local schools by 15%+, measured against comparison cohorts) AND (b) teacher-union co-design of retraining and diagnostic-support delivery (published agreements on standards as guidance, not compliance benchmarks; workload impact studies; salary progression for heterogeneous-class expertise) AND (c) binding legislation extending the comprehensive mandate to church/foundation schools or ringfencing that sector's selective intake at baseline levels. Opposition would be decisive if: the government attempts to legislate S3 without pilot evidence, or proceeds with teacher retraining designed as performance audit rather than professional development, or allows non-state selective expansion without limits.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Comprehensive schooling to age 14 as core reform model (Kockás könyv, 2016); early selection at ages 10–12 is the mechanism we oppose; compensation without structural change leaves the sorting engine intact; teacher autonomy and professional respect require structural inclusion, not remedial triage
reactions.interest: Pupils from lower-SES and rural families (whose sorting happens at age 10–12 regardless of downstream compensation); teachers in general schools tasked with 'offsetting' rather than teaching mixed-ability peers; sustainable rural school networks
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation packages that leave early selection intact do not serve equity; they entrench sorting while creating the illusion of remedy. They also devalue general-school teaching by framing it as remedial rather than primary.
reactions.argument: S4 is the scenario we oppose most fundamentally. It retains the sorting mechanism—selection at ages 10–12—that drives SES-based stratification, then proposes to 'offset' it via targeted funding and reduced retention in general schools. This is pedagogically and ethically backward. First, the scenario's own evidence status is weak on the core claim: no country has demonstrated that downstream compensation can offset sorting begun at ages 10–12. The Portuguese TEIP model (evidence: moderate) did not select as early; the offsetting assumption is unverified at Hungary's selection age and directly contradicts the scenario's admission that no such evidence exists. Second, S4 legitimises and entrenches early selection by providing a veneer of equity remedy—it converts sorting from a problem to be solved into a structural feature that is then 'compensated.' This is exactly the logic that has failed in stratified systems for decades. Third, the scenario acknowledges its own budget vulnerability: targeted funding for disadvantaged schools 'is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation,' yet the entire reform depends on sustained multi-year funding. No government has maintained such commitments across electoral cycles; the assumption of durability is not credible. Fourth, and most important for our interest: S4 leaves teachers in general schools in a position of permanent remedial triage—managing pupils sorted away from 'better' tracks and tasked with 'offsetting' rather than providing primary education. This violates teacher autonomy and respect and does not address the teacher shortage; it worsens it by signalling that general-school teaching is a lower-status second choice. S4 is not an alternative to S3; it is a way to avoid addressing the root problem.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition to S4 as a standalone reform is unconditional. However, if S4 were bundled as a transitional support package within S2 or S3 (i.e., targeted funding for general schools *during* the phase-down or transition to comprehensive schooling), it would become acceptable as a support measure, not a substitute for structural reform. The condition: S4 can be endorsed only if it is explicitly framed as a complement to, not alternative to, structural reform; its budget is legislated as multi-year mandatory spending (not discretionary); and its outcomes are measured in terms of *reducing the need for compensation* (i.e., success is when targeted funding becomes unnecessary because sorting has been reduced).
